coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid using graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid modified screen-printed carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Kunpatee, Kanjana
Traipop, Surinya
Chailapakul, Orawon
Chuanuwatanakul, Suchada
coredata.dc:description: In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ionic liquid (IL) modified screen-printed carbon electrode (GQDs/IL-SPCE) were introduced for the simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA). GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing citric acid and then dropped onto the surface of IL-SPCE, which was prepared by screen-printing the mixture of IL and carbon ink on a portable substrate. UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectrophotometry, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to characterize the synthesized GQDs and the modified electrodes. The GQDs/IL-SPCE exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of AA, DA, and UA in the mixture solution. Moreover, the anodic peak responses of these three analytes were also resolved into three well-defined peaks. Under the optimized conditions, the linear response ranges for AA, DA, and UA were 25–400 μM, 0.2–10 μM, and 0.5–20 μM, respectively, with low detection limits (σ/N = 3) of 6.64 μM, 0.06 μM, and 0.03 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor exhibited high sensitivity, low cost and successfully applied for the simultaneous detection of AA, DA, and UA in pharmaceutical products and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Dopamine
Uric acid
Screen-printed carbon electrode
Ionic liquid
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of AA, DA, and UA on the modified electrode
Effect of the amount ratio of IL to carbon ink
Effect of the amount of GQDs
Individual and simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) UV–vis and FL spectra of the synthesized GQDs. Inset of (b) shows the photographs of the GQDs solution taken under visible light (left) and UV light (right).
SEM images of (a) bare SPCE, (b) IL-SPCE, and (c) GQDs/IL-SPCE.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 and (b) Nyquist plots in the frequency range of 10–100 kHz recorded at SPCE (black), IL-SPCE (blue), and GQDs/IL-SPCE (red) in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. Inset of (b) shows an equivalent circuit of the electrode, Rs is the electrolyte resistance, Rct is the electron transfer resistance, Cdl is the double-layer capacitance, and Zw is the Warburg impedance (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
DPVs of bare SPCE, IL-SPCE, GQDs-SPCE, and GQDs/IL-SPCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) containing 400 μM AA, 10 μM DA, and 10 μM UA.

Effect of amount of IL (a) and volume of GQDs (b) on the current response of 1 mM AA, 1 mM DA, and 1 mM UA measured on GQDs/IL-SPCE using DPV in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0. Influence of pH on the anodic peak potential (c) and peak current (d) of 1 mM AA, 5 μM DA, and 10 μM UA at GQDs/IL-SPCE using DPV in 0.1 M PBS.

DPVs of the GQDs/IL-SPCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) containing (a) 1 μM DA, 5 μM UA, and AA in the concentration range of 25−400 μM; (b) 0.1 mM AA, 5 μM UA, and DA in the concentration range of 0.2−15 μM; (c) 0.1 mM AA, 1 μM DA, and UA in the concentration range of 0.5−20 μM; and (d) 25−400 μM AA, 0.2−6 μM DA, and 0.5−10 μM UA.

Schematic diagram of the preparation of GQDs/IL-SPCE and the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA using GQDs/IL-SPCE (WE: working electrode, RE: reference electrode, and CE: counter electrode).
The analytical performance for individual and simultaneous determinations of AA, DA, and UA by DPV at the GQDs/IL-SPCE.
Comparison of the analytical performance of other modified electrodes in the literature reports.
Determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid in vitamin C tablet and dopamine injection (n = 3) using our proposed method.
Simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA in a human serum sample (n = 3) using our proposed method.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid using graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid modified screen-printed carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry and Optical Spectroscopy Center of Excellent, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phayathai Road, Pathumwan, Bangkok, 10330 Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electrochemistry and Optical Spectroscopy Center of Excellent

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry and Optical Spectroscopy Center of Excellent, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phayathai Road, Pathumwan, Bangkok, 10330 Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ionic liquid (IL) modified screen-printed carbon electrode (GQDs/IL-SPCE) were introduced for the simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA). GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing citric acid and then dropped onto the surface of IL-SPCE, which was prepared by screen-printing the mixture of IL and carbon ink on a portable substrate. UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectrophotometry, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to characterize the synthesized GQDs and the modified electrodes. The GQDs/IL-SPCE exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of AA, DA, and UA in the mixture solution. Moreover, the anodic peak responses of these three analytes were also resolved into three well-defined peaks. Under the optimized conditions, the linear response ranges for AA, DA, and UA were 25–400 μM, 0.2–10 μM, and 0.5–20 μM, respectively, with low detection limits (σ/N = 3) of 6.64 μM, 0.06 μM, and 0.03 μM, respectively. The proposed sensor exhibited high sensitivity, low cost and successfully applied for the simultaneous detection of AA, DA, and UA in pharmaceutical products and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), and uric acid (UA) in a biological sample has gained considerable attention because they coexist in a living system and play important roles in a physiological process. AA, an essential component in the human diet, is a very popular antioxidant and commonly used to prevent and treat the common cold, mental illnesses and cancers [1,2]. DA is the catecholamine neurotransmitter that plays important roles in the control of a central nervous system and its abnormal levels may lead to cause various neurological diseases such as Parkinson and Schizophrenia [3,4]. UA is the main primary final product of purine metabolism. High UA concentration leads to occur some diseases such as gout, hyperuricemia, high blood pressure, and kidney disease [5]. Hence, the sensitive and selective detection method for these biomarkers is necessary for investigating their physiological functions and clinical diagnosis.

Several analytical methods including capillary electrophoresis, fluorescence spectroscopy, liquid chromatography, and chemiluminescence spectroscopy have been used for the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA [6–9]; however, these methods are time-consumption, require complicated instrument and trained personal, expensive, and unsuitable for on-site analysis. AA, DA, and UA are electroactive species; therefore, the electrochemical method for the simultaneous determination of these biomarkers has attracted much interest due to its short-time analysis, low cost, portability, high sensitivity, and selectivity. Moreover, a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) can be fabricated easily and widely used nowadays because of its excellent properties, such as low background current, wide potential window, high chemical stability, and inexpensive [10–12]. In addition, the surface of SPCEs can be easily modified to fit multiple purposes related to different analytes and to achieve a variety of improvements. These versatilities make the electrochemical analysis more convenient and portable for on-field analysis.

Nevertheless, the voltammetric oxidation peaks of these interesting compounds (AA, DA, and UA) appears at very similar potentials and difficult to distinguish at traditional electrodes. To overcome this problem, various outstanding materials such as polymers [13–16], noble metal/alloy nanoparticles [17–19], nanocomposites [20–22], ionic liquids [23,24] and carbon-based materials [25–27] were introduced as modifying materials for the electrochemical sensor. A new member of carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are graphene nanosheet in the form of a single layer or few-layer with the thickness less than 10 nm and the lateral size less than 100 nm [28]. GQDs have many excellent properties such as stable luminescence, low toxicity, high conductivity, and good biocompatibility which is demonstrated to be appropriate and efficient in both optical and electrochemical sensing applications [28,29]. On the part of the electrochemical sensor, GQDs have been employed to modify the working electrode for the sensitive and selective detection of various substances [30–32]. Ionic liquid (IL) is commonly composed of organic cations and various inorganic anions, which exists in the liquid form at room temperature. IL is one of the interesting materials used to modify electrode surface because it has many unique electrochemical properties such as high thermal stability, high ionic conductivity, wide electrochemical window, and biocompatibility. For example, IL has been proved to be an effective modifier and stabilizer for the preparation and functionalization of carbon nanotubes and noble metal nanoparticles for analysis of AA, DA, and UA [23,24].

In this study, both of the effective materials, GQDs and IL, were used to modify SPCE for highly sensitive and selective determination of AA, DA, and UA, simultaneously. The GQDs/IL modified screen-printed carbon electrode (GQDs/IL-SPCE) was characterized and then applied to the simultaneous detection of the AA, DA, and UA mixture using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The proposed modifying materials showed the increase of electrode surface and electrical conductivity, leading to the improvement of the electrochemical performance of the sensor. Moreover, this developed electrode offers an inexpensive and simple procedure to fabricate. The application of the GQDs/IL-SPCE towards the quantification of AA, DA, and UA in real samples was found to be promising.

L (+) ascorbic acid was obtained from Merck, Germany. Dopamine hydrochloride, sodium hydroxide, potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4), potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([BMIM]PF6) IL were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Uric acid was obtained from Wako Pure Chemical Industries (Osaka, Japan). Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Carlo Erba (USA). All of the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. Milli-Q water from Millipore water purification systems (Bedford, USA) with electric resistance ≥ 18.3 MΩ cm−1 was used for all experiments. Carbon ink and silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) paste 60/40 (Gwent Electronic Materials, UK) were used for screen-printing the electrodes. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with various pH values were prepared by mixing a solution of 0.1 M K2HPO4 and 0.1 M KH2PO4. A stock solution of 10 mM AA, 1 mM DA, and 1 mM UA was freshly prepared in 0.1 M PBS pH 4.0.

A photograph of the GQDs solution was acquired by a smartphone camera. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs was obtained by the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The fluorescent spectrum of synthesized GQDs was recorded with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The morphology and particle size of GQDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL, USA). All electrochemical experiments were carried out using an all-in-one screen-printed electrode consisting of (i) GQDs and IL modified carbon working electrode with projective surface area of 12.56 mm2, (ii) silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) as the pseudo-reference electrode, and (iii) carbon as the counter electrode. The electrochemical procedure was conducted using an Autolab PG 30 potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm, Netherlands) controlled by NOVA 1.10 software (Eco Chemie). The surfaces of the unmodified and modified electrodes were morphologically characterized by using a scanning electron microscope (JEOL, USA).

The GQDs were prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid according to the previous reports [33,34]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was heated to 200 °C for 5 min to obtain colorless liquid and continuously heated until the color changed to orange within 30 min. Then, the obtained orange liquid was added into 100 mL of 10 mg/mL of sodium hydroxide solution under continuous vigorous stirring. Finally, the pH value of the GQDs aqueous solution was adjusted to 8.0 with 1 mg/mL of citric acid solution. The GQDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The GQDs/IL-SPCE was in-house fabricated using a screen-printing technique as shown in Scheme 1 . Initially, the polyvinyl chloride (PVC) substrate was cut to obtain a size of 12 × 14 cm. Then, the Ag/AgCl ink was printed through the mesh onto the PVC substrate to be used as the reference electrode and dried in an oven at 55 °C for 1 h. After that, the mixture of 1.0 g carbon ink and 0.01 g [BMIM]PF6 IL was printed on top of the patterned silver layer to form a working electrode, a counter electrode, and connectors, and then dried in an oven at 55 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 7.5 μL of GQDs solution was dropped onto the surface of the working electrode and dried at room temperature. Finally, GQDs/IL-SPCE were obtained with dimensions of 2.5 × 1.0 cm for each sensor.

The electrochemical experiment was executed by dropping 60 μL of the testing solution onto the surface of the GQDs/IL-SPCE via micropipette (Scheme 1). A constant potential of +1.70 V was applied for 90 s in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 to pretreat the working electrode [35]. Subsequently, CV or DPV was performed for analysis. The optimal DPV parameters were found to be step potential of 1.5 mV, pulse amplitude of 50 mV, pulse width of 0.04 s, pulse time of 0.5 s and scanning potential between -0.4 V and 0.8 V. 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) was used as a supporting electrolyte and its background voltammogram was recorded. After that, the electrolyte solutions containing different concentrations of AA, DA, and UA were analyzed.

Vitamin C tablets (500 mg/tablet) used for the determination of AA were purchased from a local drugstore. A tablet of vitamin C was powdered and dissolved with 50 mL of water, and then filtered using a Whatman filter paper No. 1 to remove the particle matter. Subsequently, the resulting solution was diluted 100 times with the electrolyte solution and AA was determined by the standard addition method.

A 250 mg/10 mL DA injection solution was obtained from Modern Menu (Bangkok, Thailand). The sample solution was diluted with 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) and DA content was measured by DPV using optimal conditions.

The human serum sample, lyophilized human control serum sample (level 1), was purchased from Nissui Pharmaceutical (Tokyo, Japan). The serum sample was diluted with a buffer solution and used for the detection of AA, DA without any further pretreatment. To confirm the accuracy of the result, a certain amount of three analytes was spiked into the serum sample and the DPV was performed.

The morphology and particle size of as-prepared GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, TEM image displayed that the synthesized GQDs were uniform and nearly monodispersed nanoparticles, and their average size was about 5.5 nm. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy were further used to characterize the GQDs and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. UV–vis absorption spectrum (red line) exhibited a strong absorption peak at 365 nm, which assigned to the n – π* transition of C = O contained in the GQDs [36,37]. Besides, the aqueous solution of GQDs under black light (λ = 365 nm) emitted blue light as presented inset of Fig. 1b. The FL spectrum of GQDs (blue line) demonstrated the emission peak at 460 nm corresponding to the previous reports [33,37]. The obtained results suggested that the GQDs used in this study were successfully synthesized by the pyrolysis method.

The surface morphological studies of bare SPCE, IL-SPCE, and GQDs/IL-SPCE were carried out by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The surface of bare SPCE (Fig. 2 a) shows the disordered distribution of graphite flakes whereas the surface of IL-SPCE became relatively rough; demonstrating that the surface area was increased (Fig. 2b). After drop casting GQDs onto IL-SPCE, a smooth and uniform layer of GQDs film was observed, indicating that the GQDs has been highly coated on the modified electrode (Fig. 2c).

Then, SPCE, IL-SPCE, and GQDs/IL-SPCE were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) using a reference redox couple [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. CV curves in Fig. 3 a revealed that the GQDs/IL-SPCE significantly improved the redox peak currents and reduced peak separation of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− when compared with bare SPCE and IL-SPCE due to the increment of electrodes surface area by GQDs and high electrical conductivity of IL. Furthermore, the electronic transfer properties of these electrodes were studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 3b, the Nyquist plots consist of two parts, a semicircular at the higher frequency region stands for the electron transfer limited process while the diameter indicates the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), and the linear part at the lower frequency region corresponds to diffusion. Interestingly, the GQDs/IL-SPCE had the lowest Rct among all electrodes, which might be attributed to the large surface area and synergistic effect between GQDs and IL. These results are in good agreement with the CV results.

The electroactive surface areas of bare SPCE, IL-SPCE and GQDs/IL-SPCE were determined from cyclic voltammograms of 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl at various scan rates (Fig. S1) using Randles-Sevcik equation [38].
Ip = (2.69 × 105) A D1/2 n3/2 ν 1/2 C

where A is the electrode surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1), n is the number of electrons involved in the process (n = 1), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM), ν is the scan rate (V s−1), and Ip is the intensity of peak current (A). The electroactive surface area values of the bare SPCE, IL-SPCE, and GQDs/IL-SPCE were calculated to be 0.0027, 0.0040, and 0.0056 mm2, respectively. These results indicate that the GQDs/IL-SPCE provided a larger electroactive area, therefore, GQDs and IL are promising materials that can improve the electron transfer ability and sensitivity of the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of AA, DA, and UA on the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the mixture of AA, DA, and UA in 0.1 M PBS pH 4.0 on the different modified electrode was investigated by DPV. As seen in Fig. 4 , the oxidation peak of AA and DA on bare SPCE was overlapped together and the small peak of UA was appeared at 0.38 V, representing the poor electrochemical activity. Meanwhile, the separated peaks of three components were observed at potentials of 0.02, 0.16, and 0.32 V on IL-SPCE due to high electrical conductivity and large effective surface area of IL leading to the enhanced electrocatalytic activity of the electrode surface [39]. On GQDs-SPCE, the oxidation peaks of AA, DA, and UA were located around -0.02, 0.15, and 0.29 V, respectively, with the current response of DA was significantly increased. The results can be attributed to the π–π interaction between the phenyl structure of DA and the hexagonal carbon structure of GQDs. Moreover, the peak potentials for AA and UA were negatively shifted compared with those on the IL-SPCE. This may be a result of the different types of hydrogen bonding between the GQDs functional group and AA or UA that improve selectivity [40–42]. However, IL-SPCE and GQDs-SPCE were not good enough for measuring these three species. When both GQDs and IL were used to modify on the carbon electrode surface, the well-defined and distinguishable sharp peaks of three electroactive species appeared at -0.02, 0.18, and 0.36 V, respectively. The larger separation of oxidation peak potentials of AADA, DAUA, and AAUA (200, 180, and 380 mV, respectively) was obtained as compared with those on the GQDs-SPCE (170, 140, and 310 mV). Furthermore, oxidation peak currents were remarkably increased on GQDs/IL-SPCE. The results indicated that the GQDs/IL-SPCE combined the excellent electrochemical properties of IL and GQDs. Therefore, the simultaneous determination of three interested analytes can be realized on this electrode.

In order to evaluate the electrochemical process of AA, DA, and UA on the GQDs/IL-SPCE, the CV response of these three analytes at various scan rates (10−70 mV/s) were studied (Fig. S2). The anodic peak currents of AA, DA, and UA were linearly proportional to the square root of scan rate via the linear regression equation of Ip,AA (μA) = 1.7814υ1/2 (mV/s) – 0.2702 (R2 = 0.9921), Ip,DA (μA) = 3.8934υ1/2 (mV/s) – 1.4229 (R2 = 0.9971), and Ip,UA (μA) = 3.3935υ1/2 (mV/s) + 1.9151 (R2 = 0.9935). These results demonstrated that the oxidation reactions of AA, DA, and UA on the GQDs/IL-SPCE were diffusion-controlled processes.

To further improve the analytical performance of the fabricated electrochemical sensor, the effects of operational parameters including the amount ratio of IL to carbon ink, amount of GQDs, and pH value of electrolyte solution were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount ratio of IL to carbon ink
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the amount ratio of IL to carbon ink was examined in the range of 0.0–3.0 % w/w as shown in Fig. 5 a. It was observed that the oxidation peak current of 1 mM AA, DA, and UA increased with increasing the amount of IL until it reached a maximum value at 1.0 % w/w and then apparently decreased. This can be explained that the overload of IL content significantly enhanced capacitance or background current of the modified electrode [43,44]. Fig. S3 shows CV responses of bare SPCE and IL-SPCE in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0. It can be seen that the background current of SPCE has significantly increased after modified with IL. Therefore, the optimal amount ratio of IL to carbon ink was 1 % w/w and the following experiments were carried out at this ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the volume of GQDs solution over the range 0.0–10.0 μL on the electrochemical response of 1 mM AA, DA, and UA are shown in Fig. 5b. As the amount of GQDs increased, the anodic peak current of AA did not change significantly due to poor π-π interaction between AA and GQDs, whereas the currents response of DA and UA increased up to the maximum value at 7.5 μL and then decreased with further increases in the amount of GQDs. This decrease in peak current was probably caused by the high amount of GQDs leading to the aggregation of GQDs. Therefore, the GQDs solution of 7.5 μL was selected as an optimal parameter for further study.

The influence of pH on the anodic peak current and peak potential of 1 mM AA, 5 μM DA, and 10 μM UA at the modified electrode was studied in the range from pH 2.0 to pH 9.0. Fig. 5c shows that the anodic peak potentials of these analytes shifted negatively with the higher pH values because a deprotonation step in the electrocatalytic oxidation process occurs more easily with increasing pH [45,46]. Moreover, the relationship between peak potential and the pH value was obtained as follows: Epa,AA (V) = 0.2139−0.0460 pH (R2 = 0.946), Epa,DA (V) = 0.4498−0.0558 pH (R2 = 0.968), and Epa,UA (V) = 0.6283−0.0617 pH (R2 = 0.970). The slopes of AA, DA, and UA were -46.0, -55.8, and -61.7 mV/pH, respectively, which were close to the theoretical value (−59 mV/pH) demonstrating the electrode process is two-proton and two-electron transfer process [47]. The maximum separation of oxidation peak potentials of AA–DA, DA–UA, and AA-UA were observed at pH 4.0 (Fig. S4). Fig. 5d illustrated that the peak currents of these analytes also depended on the pH value of buffer solution and 0.1 M PBS pH 4.0 was chosen as the supporting electrolyte solution in this experiment with the high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the analytical utility of the GQDs/IL-SPCE, the individual and simultaneous determinations of the mixture AA, DA, and UA were investigated by DPV. For individual measurement, the concentration of one analyte was changed, while those of the other two compounds were kept constant. As seen in Fig. 6 a–c, the peak potentials of these three species at the GQDs/IL-SPCE were well-separated and the anodic peak current of each species was directly proportional to its concentrations. These results demonstrated that changing the concentration of an analyte has no obvious influence on the detection of the other two compounds. In addition, DPV was also carried out at GQDs/IL-SPCE as changing concentration of AA (25−400 μM), DA (0.2−6 μM), and UA (0.5−10 μM), simultaneously. Fig. 6d shows that the three anodic peaks were well-separated and the peak currents of all analytes increased proportionally to their concentrations. Under the optimal conditions, the analytical performance including linear range, linear equation, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantitation (LOQ) for the individual and simultaneous detections of AA, DA, and UA using the proposed method are summarized in Table 1 .

The analytical performances of the proposed DPV method on GQDs/IL-SPCE compared with the other works are displayed in Table 2 . It was found that the developed GQDs/IL-SPCE could be applied to individually and simultaneously determine of AA, DA, and UA in biological fluids with the wide linear range and low detection limits.

To analyze the reproducibility of the proposed method, the electrochemical response of a mixture of 300 μM AA, 10 μM DA, and 5 μM UA in 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) was examined by five different GQDs/IL-SPCEs. It was found that the obtained peak responses remained the same in the detection with different sensors (Fig. S5). The relative standard deviation (RSD) in peak currents of AA, DA, and UA were found to be 4.15, 3.38, and 3.47 %, respectively, indicating that the modified electrode had good reproducibility. In addition, the stability of the GQDs/IL-SPCE was investigated. The modified electrode was stored in a desiccator. After five days, the current responses of AA, DA, and UA retained 83.24, 80.08, and 81.50 % of its initial current response, indicating good stability of the GQDs/IL-SPCE (Fig. S6). The selectivity of this method was evaluated by testing a solution of 100 μM AA, 10 μM DA and 5 μM UA in the presence of potentially interfering compounds including citric acid, glucose, l-cysteine, NaCl, KCl, MgSO4, CaCl2, and Zn(NO3)2. The results demonstrated that 1000 μM of these compounds had no significant interference (signal change < 5%) in the determination of these analytes (Fig. S7). Consequently, the proposed method offered a highly selective for the determination of the mixture of AA, DA, and UA.

To investigate the performance of the developed electrode in real samples, the GQDs/IL-SPCE was used to directly analyze AA in Vitamin C tablet and DA in Dopamine injection using standard addition method. The analytical results are summarized in Table 3 . It can be seen that the results from the proposed method were in good agreement with those from labeled amounts (percentage error less than ± 5% and the paired t-test at 95 % confidence level gave tcal (0.34 for AA and 0.54 for DA) below tcrit at 2.776 with 4 degrees of freedom).

Moreover, the developed method was also used to the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA in human serum, which was diluted for 500 times with 0.1 M PBS (pH 4.0) before detection. The diluted sample mentioned above was spiked with a certain amount of the three analytes and then analyzed by the standard addition method. The recoveries of the spiked sample were found in the range of 99.04–106.65 % (Table 4 ). These results indicated that the application of the developed method was successfully applied for individually and simultaneously detections of AA, DA, and UA in pharmaceutical product and biological sample.

A novel electrochemical method based on GQDs and IL modified SPCE was successfully developed for the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, and UA. It can be clearly concluded that the GQDs/IL-SPCE exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of the interesting compounds and also resolved the oxidation peak responses of these three analytes into three well-defined peaks, which could be attributed to the high surface area and high electrical conductivity of GQDs and IL. Furthermore, the proposed method showed high sensitivity, good linear range, low detection limit, good reproducibility, high selectivity and great potential application for the individual and simultaneous determinations of AA, DA, and UA.

We proposed both of the effective materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ionic liquid (IL), to modify SPCE for highly sensitive and selective determination of AA, DA, and UA, simultaneously. The GQDs/IL modified screen-printed carbon electrode (GQDs/IL-SPCE) was characterized and then applied to simultaneous detection of the AA, DA, and UA mixture using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The proposed modifying materials, GQDs and IL, showed the increasing of electrode surface and conductivity, leading to improve electrochemical performance of the sensor. Thus, the application of the GQDs/IL-SPCE towards the quantification of AA, DA, and UA in real samples was found to be promising.

Kanjana Kunpatee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Surinya Traipop: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Orawon Chailapakul: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Suchada Chuanuwatanakul: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the 90th Anniversary of Chulalongkorn University Fund (Ratchadaphiseksomphot Endowment Fund), Thailand Research Fund through Research Team Promotion Grant (RTA60800002), and the Electrochemistry and Optical Spectroscopy Center of Excellence, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical methods for ascorbic acid determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Pisoschi, A. Pop, A.I. Serban, C. Fafaneata, Electrochemical methods for ascorbic acid determination, Electrochim. Acta 121 (2014) 443-460. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2013.12.127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of ascorbate and dehydroascorbate contents in biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Koshiishi, T. Imanari, Measurement of Ascorbate and Dehydroascorbate Contents in Biological Fluids, Anal. Chem. 69 (1997) 216-220. https://doi.org/10.1021/ac960704k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How addictive drugs disrupt presynaptic dopamine neurotransmission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Sulzer, How addictive drugs disrupt presynaptic dopamine neurotransmission, Neuron 69 (2011) 628-49. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2011.02.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The fate of striatal dopaminergic neurons in Parkinson’s disease and Huntington’s chorea

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Huot, M. Lévesque, A. Parent, The fate of striatal dopaminergic neurons in Parkinson's disease and Huntington's chorea, Brain 130 (2006) 222-232. https://doi.org/10.1093/brain/awl332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of serum uric acid using the optical polymeric enzyme biochip system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Huang, Y.C. Shih, C.Y. Wu, C.J. Yuan, Y.S. Yang, Y.K. Li, T.K. Wu, Detection of serum uric acid using the optical polymeric enzyme biochip system, Biosens. Bioelectron. 19 (2004) 1627-1633. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2003.12.026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amperometric PDMS/glass capillary electrophoresis-based biosensor microchip for catechol and dopamine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Schöning, M. Jacobs, A. Muck, D.T. Knobbe, J. Wang, M. Chatrathi, S. Spillmann, Amperometric PDMS/glass capillary electrophoresis-based biosensor microchip for catechol and dopamine detection, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 108 (2005) 688-694. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2004.11.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: β-cyclodextrin enhanced fluorimetry for a determination of ascorbic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yang, C. Sun, X. Wu, Y. Diao, β-cyclodextrin enhanced fluorimetry for a determination of ascorbic acid, Anal. Lett. 34 (2001) 1331-1339. https://doi.org/10.1081/AL-100104157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of uric acid in human urine and serum by capillary electrophoresis with chemiluminescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, J. Wang, F. Ye, Y.M. Liu, Determination of uric acid in human urine and serum by capillary electrophoresis with chemiluminescence detection, Anal. Biochem. 378 (2008) 127-31. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2008.04.014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of creatinine and uric acid in urine by liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry with polarity switching electrospray ionization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Kwon, J.Y. Kim, S. Suh, M.K. In, Simultaneous determination of creatinine and uric acid in urine by liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry with polarity switching electrospray ionization, Forensic Sci. Int. 221 (2012) 57-64. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.forsciint.2012.03.025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in the field of screen-printed electrodes and their related applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.D. Renedo, M.A. Alonso-Lomillo, M.J.A. Martínez, Recent developments in the field of screen-printed electrodes and their related applications, Talanta 73 (2007) 202-219. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2007.03.050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improvement of electrochemical performance of screen-printed carbon electrodes by UV/ozone modification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, Z. Xu, M. Zhang, J. Liu, H. Zou, L. Wang, Improvement of electrochemical performance of screen-printed carbon electrodes by UV/ozone modification, Talanta 192 (2019) 40-45. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2018.08.065.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reduced graphene oxide-cyclodextrin-platinum nanocomposite modified screen printed electrode for the detection of cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Singh, N. Jaiswal, I. Tiwari, C.W. Foster, C.E. Banks, A reduced graphene oxide-cyclodextrin-platinum nanocomposite modified screen printed electrode for the detection of cysteine, J. Electroanal. Chem. 829 (2018) 230-240. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.09.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of uric acid, dopamine and ascorbic acid based on poly(bromocresol green) modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ouyang, L. Luo, Y. Ding, B. Liu, D. Xu, A. Huang, Simultaneous determination of uric acid, dopamine and ascorbic acid based on poly(bromocresol green) modified glassy carbon electrode, J. Electroanal. Chem. 748 (2015) 1-7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2015.04.026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous electroanalysis of dopamine and ascorbic acid using poly (N,N-dimethylaniline)-modified electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.R. Roy, T. Okajima, T. Ohsaka, Simultaneous electroanalysis of dopamine and ascorbic acid using poly (N,N-dimethylaniline)-modified electrodes, Bioelectrochemistry 59 (2003) 11-19. https://doi.org/10.1016/S1567-5394(02)00156-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, uric acid and dopamine at modified electrode based on hybrid nickel hexacyanoferrate/poly(1,5-diaminonaphthalene)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Hathoot, K.M. Hassan, W.A. Essa, M. Abdel-Azzem, Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, uric acid and dopamine at modified electrode based on hybrid nickel hexacyanoferrate/poly(1,5-diaminonaphthalene), J. Iran. Chem. Soc. 14 (2017) 1789-1799. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13738-017-1119-8.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Chandra, B.E. Kumara Swamy, O. Gilbert, B.S. Sherigara, Voltammetric detection of dopamine in presence of ascorbic acid and uric acid at poly (xylenol orange) film-coated graphite pencil electrode, Int. J. Electrochem. 2011 (2011) 1-8. https://dx.doi.org/10.4061/2011/512692.

Du, A facile electrochemical sensor based on reduced graphene oxide and Au nanoplates modified glassy carbon electrode for simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid, Sens. Acta 168 (2015) 191-198. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2015.03.215.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kanjana Kunpatee is a master student at Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University. Her research focuses on development of electrochemical sensor based on nanomaterials.

Surinya Traipop is a postdoctoral researcher at Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University. Her research fields are electrochemical and analytical development for organic and inorganic compounds.

Orawon Chailapakul is a Professor at Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University. Her research interests include electroanalytical detection, flow-based system and lap-on-chip, devices for various applications, immunoassay, and battery.

Suchada Chuanuwatanakul is an Associate Professor at Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University. Her current research topics are development and application of nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors for electroanalytical detections of various compounds, development and application of flow-based electroanalytical methods to food and environmental analysis.
